name: Guinea-Bissau Portuguese

ancestors.notes: European Portuguese colonial base. Guinea-Bissau was a Portuguese colony until 1974.

Guinea-Bissau Creole (Kriol/GBC) adstrate: the true lingua franca, spoken as L1 by ~44% and as L2 by most of the population. Descended from Portuguese; exerts strong influence on the local Portuguese register.
Fula (Fulah) substrate: one of the major languages of Guinea-Bissau (Pulaar-speaking communities in the east); phonological transfer into Portuguese.

notes: Guinea-Bissau Portuguese (PortuguĂŞs da GuinĂŠ-Bissau). Official language; spoken as L1 by a small educated elite (<10%); Guinea-Bissau Creole (Kriol) is the actual national lingua franca. Portuguese functions mainly in formal, governmental, and educational domains. KEY FEATURES: (1) FIVE-VOWEL SYSTEM: /É¨/ absent; full articulation of unstressed vowels. (2) SIBILANTS: alveolar [s] in coda (no palatalisation). (3) RHOTIC: alveolar trill [r]. (4) CREOLE TRANSFER: strong lexical and syntactic influence from Kriol; many speakers are effectively L2 users of Portuguese. (5) Very limited documentation of phonological features. Sources: Couto (1994), Kihm (1994), PALOP linguistic studies.

allophone_rules.notes: Guinea-Bissau Portuguese keeps an alveolar coda /s/ (no Lisbon 'chiado'); this overrides the inherited pt-PT PT_CODA_S_HUSH rule by id, matching the alveolar coda already declared in this spec's positional_graphemes.
Guinea-Bissau Portuguese keeps an alveolar coda /z/; overrides the inherited pt-PT PT_CODA_Z_HUSH rule by id.

stress.notes: Acordo OrtogrĂĄfico 1990 conventions: paroxytone default; written accents override; final -i/-u/-r/-l/-z/-x/-im/-om/-um/-ns and nasal diphthongs attract final stress. Unmarked -em/-am stay paroxytone (homem, falam). DIPHTHONGS: declared so the bundled syllabifier counts nuclei correctly â without them every vowel run merges into one nucleus and co-e-lho is read as coe-lho, one syllable short, putting the stress and its vowel reduction on the wrong vowel. â¨oeâŠ is hiatus, not a diphthong (Cunha & Cintra, Â§Ditongos).
